message:
Refactor signature payload to use a view struct and flatten verifier economy eviction logic

Verifier SDK signature payload refactor (verifier_sdk.rs):
- Replace the 8-parameter positional call to
  verification_result_signature_payload() with a
  VerificationResultSignatureView struct that groups all fields into a
  named, lifetime-annotated view. This eliminates parameter-ordering bugs
  (the old signature had 5 consecutive &str parameters that could easily
  be transposed without a type error) and makes call sites self-documenting
  via named fields.
- Update both call sites (build_signed_verification_result and
  verify_verification_result_signature) to construct the view struct.
- Apply rustfmt formatting fix to a multi-line assertion in the
  verify_migration_artifact test.

Verifier economy eviction cleanup (verifier_economy/mod.rs):
- Flatten nested if-let blocks in register_verifier() and
  publish_attestation() by chaining the eviction remove into the existing
  let-chain guard. This replaces a pattern where the remove was inside an
  inner if-let with a single cohesive let-chain that reads top-to-bottom:
  check capacity → find oldest key → remove it → clean up indices.
  Behavior is identical — the inner if-let could never fail because the
  key was just obtained from the map — but the flattened version makes
  that invariant structurally obvious.

Update issue tracker metadata.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1nk5","title":"[10.13] Add SSRF-deny default policy template (localhost/tailnet/private CIDR denied unless explicitly allowed).","description":"Master Plan Source: PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKEN_NODE.md
Section: 10.13 — FCP Deep-Mined Expansion Execution Track (9I)

Why This Exists:
Deep connector/provider contract track: lifecycle FSMs, conformance harnesses, fencing, sandboxing, revocation freshness, and protocol hardening.

Task Objective:
Add SSRF-deny default policy template (localhost/tailnet/private CIDR denied unless explicitly allowed).

Acceptance Criteria:
- Default templates block unsafe internal destinations; explicit allowlist exceptions require policy receipts; regression tests cover common SSRF patterns.

Expected Artifacts:
- `config/policies/network_guard_default.toml`, `tests/security/ssrf_default_deny.rs`, `artifacts/10.13/ssrf_policy_test_report.json`.

- Machine-readable verification artifact at `artifacts/section_10_13/bd-1nk5/verification_evidence.json` for CI/release gating.
- Human-readable verification summary at `artifacts/section_10_13/bd-1nk5/verification_summary.md` linking implementation intent to measured outcomes.
Testing & Logging Requirements:
- Unit tests for core logic, invariants, and error handling.
- Integration/E2E tests for end-to-end control-flow behavior.
- Detailed structured logs/telemetry with stable codes and trace correlation IDs.
- Deterministic replay fixture or reproducible artifact for failure analysis.

Task-Specific Clarification:
- For "[10.13] Add SSRF-deny default policy template (localhost/tailnet/private CIDR denied unless explicitly allowed).", preserve full capability scope from the plan; no feature compression or silent omission is allowed.
- Verification for "[10.13] Add SSRF-deny default policy template (localhost/tailnet/private CIDR denied unless explicitly allowed)." must include capability-specific thresholds and machine-readable pass/fail evidence artifacts.
- Unit tests, integration/E2E scripts, and structured logs for "[10.13] Add SSRF-deny default policy template (localhost/tailnet/private CIDR denied unless explicitly allowed)." must support deterministic replay and root-cause triage without hidden context.

Why This Improves User Outcomes:
- "[10.13] Add SSRF-deny default policy template (localhost/tailnet/private CIDR denied unless explicitly allowed)." is required to reduce operator error, increase deterministic safety guarantees, and improve time-to-diagnosis for failures in real-world workflows.
- Explicit success/failure evidence for "[10.13] Add SSRF-deny default policy template (localhost/tailnet/private CIDR denied unless explicitly allowed)." prevents false confidence and improves trust in migration/compatibility/security outcomes.
- Detailed unit + integration/E2E + structured logging requirements ensure reproducible quality and faster incident remediation for users.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:52.366422143Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T11:28:37.880582192Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T11:28:37.880556835Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-13","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1nk5","depends_on_id":"bd-2m2b","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:43:12.756576197Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1nl1","title":"[10.17] Build proof-carrying speculative execution governance framework for extension-host hot paths.","description":"Master Plan Source: PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKEN_NODE.md

Task Objective:
Build proof-carrying speculative execution governance framework for extension-host hot paths.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Speculative transforms cannot activate without proof receipts and guard checks; guard failure always degrades to deterministic safe baseline with no correctness regression; activation occurs only via approved franken_engine interfaces.

Expected Artifacts:

- Integration/E2E tests for end-to-end control-flow behavior.
- Detailed structured logs/telemetry with stable codes and trace correlation IDs.
- Deterministic replay fixture or reproducible artifact for failure analysis.

- Verification for "[10.17] Build proof-carrying speculative execution governance framework for extension-host hot paths." must include capability-specific thresholds and machine-readable pass/fail evidence artifacts.
- Unit tests, integration/E2E scripts, and structured logs for "[10.17] Build proof-carrying speculative execution governance framework for extension-host hot paths." must support deterministic replay and root-cause triage without hidden context.

Why This Improves User Outcomes:
- "[10.17] Build proof-carrying speculative execution governance framework for extension-host hot paths." is required to reduce operator error, increase deterministic safety guarantees, and improve time-to-diagnosis for failures in real-world workflows.
- Explicit success/failure evidence for "[10.17] Build proof-carrying speculative execution governance framework for extension-host hot paths." prevents false confidence and improves trust in migration/compatibility/security outcomes.
- Detailed unit + integration/E2E + structured logging requirements ensure reproducible quality and faster incident remediation for users.","acceptance_criteria":"- Speculative transforms cannot activate without proof receipts and guard checks; guard failure always degrades to deterministic safe baseline with no correctness regression; activation occurs only via approved franken_engine interfaces.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"DarkLantern","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:37:02.927493339Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T04:14:51.994167070Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T04:14:51.994141452Z","close_reason":"Proof-carrying speculation governance delivered with guard/fallback contract and verification artifacts","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-17","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1nl1","depends_on_id":"bd-1ta","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:46:33.798018723Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1nl1","depends_on_id":"bd-229","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:46:33.843240390Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1nl1","depends_on_id":"bd-3il","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:46:33.888249500Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1nl1","depends_on_id":"bd-3qo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:46:33.933893504Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1nl1","depends_on_id":"bd-5rh","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:46:33.978872949Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1nl1","depends_on_id":"bd-cda","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:46:34.023526217Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1nl1","depends_on_id":"bd-n71","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:46:34.070551974Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1nmg","title":"Epic: CI/CD Pipeline Setup","description":"- type: task","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:47:58.072163029Z","updated_at":"2026-02-20T07:49:21.083126433Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T07:49:21.083109191Z","close_reason":"Superseded by canonical detailed master-plan graph (bd-33v) with full 10.N-10.21 and 11-16 coverage, explicit dependencies, and per-section verification gates.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"bd-1now","title":"[runtime] Selective Asupersync leverage plan for franken_node","description":"Why this exists:
A focused architecture audit on 2026-03-09 asked whether `franken_node` should leverage Asupersync more aggressively for materially better performance, correctness, and robustness.

Key findings from that audit:
- `frankenengine-node` still declares `tokio`, but the only direct Tokio usage in this crate is `#[tokio::main]` on `crates/franken-node/src/main.rs`; the crate currently has no `.await` sites. That means Tokio is currently acting as dead executor scaffolding rather than a meaningful runtime substrate.
- The broader system already consumes canonical Asupersync primitives indirectly through `frankenengine-engine`, whose `control_plane` adapter imports canonical `/dp/asupersync`-owned types such as `Cx`, `Budget`, and `TraceId`. So the project already benefits from the real Asupersync stack upstream of this crate.
- `franken_node` itself contains multiple local semantic/model layers that speak in Asupersync-like concepts (`ControlPlaneCx`, region ownership, supervision, budget guards, deterministic labs), but many of those surfaces are model/spec/data-structure layers rather than live async runtime boundaries.
- The one clearly high-value runtime seam is the telemetry ingestion path: `TelemetryBridge` plus `EngineDispatcher` currently use background `thread::spawn`, nested per-connection threads, and `Arc<Mutex<FrankensqliteAdapter>>`. That is the seam where explicit ownership, bounded work admission, structured shutdown, and supervised lifecycle semantics could plausibly produce a real reliability win.

Strategic decision encoded by this epic:
- Do not launch a broad crate-wide Asupersync migration just because local modules use Asupersync-flavored vocabulary.
- Remove clearly dead runtime scaffolding where there is near-zero downside.
- Add guardrails so ambient executor creep does not return by accident.
- Concentrate any serious runtime-ownership work on the telemetry ingestion seam, because that is the place where long-lived work, backpressure, failure handling, and shutdown semantics actually matter.
- Record explicit deferred trigger-beads for future native Asupersync adoption at HTTP/gRPC boundaries and actor-style state ownership boundaries, so future work is driven by real topology and not by aesthetic migration pressure.

Non-goals:
- Do not rewrite the whole crate around Asupersync right now.
- Do not force native Asupersync service/request-region APIs onto `api/service.rs` before this crate owns a real async HTTP or gRPC boundary.
- Do not replace every `Mutex`/singleton with actors just for stylistic purity; only do so where concurrency topology, ownership, restart policy, or reply obligations justify it.
- Do not duplicate canonical upstream primitive ownership already centralized in `frankenengine-engine` without an explicit reason.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The tracker contains a complete, self-contained implementation graph for selective Asupersync leverage in `franken_node`, including immediate work, deferred trigger conditions, and the reasoning that led to those choices.
2. The graph distinguishes clearly between: (a) immediate high-confidence cleanup, (b) likely high-value runtime-ownership refactors, and (c) future migrations that should remain deferred until the crate grows the required topology.
3. The graph captures enough architectural context that future contributors do not need to reopen this chat or any external markdown plan to understand why these tasks exist, what problem they solve, and why broader migration is intentionally constrained.","notes":"Audit evidence that motivated this epic:
- `crates/franken-node/src/main.rs`: `#[tokio::main]` on the CLI bootstrap, with no `.await` usage discovered in the crate during the audit.
- `crates/franken-node/Cargo.toml` and workspace `Cargo.toml`: explicit `tokio` dependency still present.
- `crates/franken-node/src/ops/telemetry_bridge.rs`: background listener built from `thread::spawn`, nested per-connection threads, and `Arc<Mutex<FrankensqliteAdapter>>`.
- `crates/franken-node/src/ops/engine_dispatcher.rs`: telemetry bridge started as background side-effect before engine process dispatch, with no explicit stop/join handle returned to the caller.
- `/data/projects/franken_engine/crates/franken-engine/src/control_plane/mod.rs`: canonical control-plane adapter already imports `/dp/asupersync` primitives (`Cx`, `Budget`, `TraceId`, etc.), so direct value from additional node-side migration must be justified rather than assumed.
- `crates/franken-node/src/connector/region_ownership.rs`, `crates/franken-node/src/connector/supervision.rs`, and `crates/franken-node/src/runtime/region_tree.rs`: local semantic twins exist and need explicit policy, not accidental semantic drift.

This epic is intentionally decision-heavy because the main risk here is future churn without leverage.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:39.683448788Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:39.870705857Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","planning","runtime"]}
+{"id":"bd-1now.1","title":"Document selective Asupersync leverage baseline and decision boundaries","description":"Why this exists:
Before any runtime change lands, the project needs a self-contained record of what the 2026-03-09 audit actually found and what it did not find. Without that, future work risks turning into cargo-cult migration or re-litigating the same decision repeatedly.

What this task must do:
Create the canonical decision record for selective Asupersync leverage in `franken_node`.

The record must capture:
- the exact direct Tokio footprint in `frankenengine-node` at audit time,
- the exact indirect Asupersync footprint already entering through `frankenengine-engine`,
- the difference between local model/spec layers and live runtime boundaries,
- the reason the telemetry listener path was selected as the main high-value runtime seam,
- the explicit reasons broad migration of `api/service.rs` and similar skeletons is deferred,
- the criteria future contributors must use before widening Asupersync adoption.

Why this matters:
This bead is the anchor that keeps future implementation work from silently drifting into “replace things because they look old” instead of “replace only where ownership/lifecycle semantics buy correctness.”","acceptance_criteria":"1. The decision record inventories the direct Tokio footprint and confirms whether the executor shell is functionally dead or still required.
2. The decision record inventories the indirect canonical Asupersync imports already present upstream in `frankenengine-engine` and explains why that existing leverage changes the adoption strategy in `franken_node`.
3. The decision record classifies relevant local surfaces into at least: live runtime seam, local semantic/model layer, skeleton/future boundary, and deferred migration candidate.
4. The record explicitly names the telemetry ingestion path as the main immediate refactor candidate and explains why other surfaces are deferred.
5. The record is self-contained and detailed enough that future contributors can make adoption decisions without consulting this chat.","notes":"Important background to preserve:
- This is not a neutral “survey every Asupersync feature” task. It is a selective-leverage decision task.
- The bar for recommending broader adoption is “blatantly obvious and materially accretive for performance/correctness/robustness,” not “technically possible.”
- If this task concludes that some surfaces should remain local models or be explicitly deferred, that is a valid success case.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:40.078802649Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:40.271051369Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","audit","runtime"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:40.078802649Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1now.2","title":"Remove dead Tokio bootstrap from frankenengine-node CLI","description":"Why this exists:
The audit found an explicit `tokio` dependency and `#[tokio::main]` bootstrap in `frankenengine-node`, but no `.await` sites anywhere in the crate. That strongly suggests Tokio is currently dead executor scaffolding rather than a necessary runtime boundary.

What this task must do:
Remove the dead Tokio bootstrap from the `frankenengine-node` CLI if the audit confirmation still holds.

Scope:
- Prove the crate really does not require Tokio for any live async behavior.
- Convert the CLI bootstrap to a synchronous `main` if that proof holds.
- Remove the direct `tokio` dependency from `crates/franken-node/Cargo.toml` and the workspace root if this workspace no longer needs it.
- Ensure the resulting bootstrap remains straightforward and does not introduce a fake local executor abstraction just to preserve shape.

Why this matters:
This is the lowest-risk, highest-confidence cleanup in the audit. It removes ambient runtime machinery that currently adds conceptual noise without buying structured concurrency, performance, or correctness.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The crate either proves Tokio is still needed and documents the exact surviving requirement, or it removes the Tokio bootstrap entirely.
2. If removed, `crates/franken-node/src/main.rs` no longer uses `#[tokio::main]`, and the crate compiles/runs without direct Tokio bootstrap support.
3. `crates/franken-node/Cargo.toml` and workspace `Cargo.toml` no longer carry Tokio solely for this dead bootstrap path.
4. Validation demonstrates that no hidden `.await`/Tokio runtime dependence was accidentally masked by the refactor.
5. The resulting change is framed explicitly as dead-runtime cleanup, not as “Asupersync migration complete.”","notes":"This task should stay small and surgical. If investigation discovers a real async requirement, stop pretending this is trivial and document the blocker clearly.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:40.473235864Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.097728479Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","runtime","tokio-cleanup"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:40.473235864Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.097671102Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1now.3","title":"Add guardrail against ambient Tokio/runtime reintroduction","description":"Why this exists:
If the dead Tokio bootstrap is removed, the project still needs a future-proof guardrail so contributors do not casually reintroduce an ambient executor shell when adding unrelated features.

What this task must do:
Add a regression gate that makes the runtime-boundary rule explicit: `frankenengine-node` should not regain direct Tokio bootstrap/runtime usage unless the crate grows a real async boundary that justifies it.

Scope:
- Add a lightweight static checker, test, or CI-visible invariant covering the banned pattern(s).
- Document the exception rule: reintroduction is allowed only when the crate owns a real async boundary and the architectural decision record is updated accordingly.
- Tie the guardrail to the broader selective-adoption strategy so it is obvious that this is about preventing ambient runtime drift, not about banning future work.

Why this matters:
Without a guardrail, dead executor scaffolding tends to creep back in one convenience macro at a time.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The repo has a deterministic check that fails if `frankenengine-node` reintroduces dead Tokio bootstrap/runtime scaffolding without an explicit architectural exception.
2. The check documents the permitted exception case: a real async boundary with updated decision record and explicit justification.
3. The guardrail is cheap enough to run routinely and precise enough to avoid false confidence.","notes":"This task is intentionally coupled to the bootstrap cleanup. If Tokio remains for a real reason, adapt the guardrail to ban only unjustified executor creep rather than blindly banning the dependency name.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:40.876029147Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.507520915Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","runtime","tokio-cleanup","verification-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:40.876029147Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.302404126Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.507463948Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1now.4","title":"TelemetryBridge selective Asupersync adoption cluster","description":"Why this exists:
The audit identified the telemetry ingestion path as the one obviously high-value runtime seam in `franken_node`.

Current shape:
- `crates/franken-node/src/ops/telemetry_bridge.rs` starts a background listener with `thread::spawn`.
- Each accepted connection spawns another background thread.
- Persistence is shared through `Arc<Mutex<FrankensqliteAdapter>>`.
- `crates/franken-node/src/ops/engine_dispatcher.rs` starts the bridge as a side effect before dispatching the engine process, but does not surface an explicit stop/join lifecycle handle back to the caller.

Why this is the right selective-adoption target:
This seam has the exact properties Asupersync is strongest at: owned long-lived work, lifecycle obligations, shutdown sequencing, backpressure, and deterministic failure reasoning. If any runtime area in this crate deserves a stronger ownership/supervision story, it is this one.

Goal:
Plan and execute a surgical migration of the telemetry listener toward explicit owned-worker/supervised semantics, without forcing a broad crate-wide rewrite.

Non-goals:
- Do not use this as a pretext to rewrite unrelated runtime/model modules.
- Do not add Asupersync dependencies casually if a local owned-worker implementation with equivalent semantics is the right fit.
- Do not preserve the existing nested-thread shape just because it currently “works.”","acceptance_criteria":"1. The telemetry ingestion path has an explicit ownership/lifecycle contract rather than incidental background thread behavior.
2. Work admission, shutdown, error propagation, and persistence ownership are explicit and testable.
3. The resulting architecture is justified as a focused, high-value runtime improvement rather than a symbolic migration.","notes":"This cluster is where Asupersync-style structured concurrency/supervision ideas are most likely to pay off immediately.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:41.273377685Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.708413913Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","runtime","telemetry"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:41.273377685Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.708343772Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1now.4.1","title":"Specify TelemetryBridge ownership, backpressure, and lifecycle contract","description":"Why this exists:
TelemetryBridge should not be refactored by intuition alone. The current path mixes accept-loop ownership, connection handling, persistence ownership, and process lifecycle concerns. A spec-first contract is needed before any implementation churn.

What this task must do:
Define the target ownership/lifecycle contract for telemetry ingestion.

The contract must answer:
- What owns the listener?
- What owns per-connection read loops?
- Whether per-connection concurrency is bounded, serialized, or pooled.
- What owns `FrankensqliteAdapter` writes.
- What the explicit lifecycle handle looks like (`start`, `stop`, `join`, error reporting).
- How backpressure behaves when the engine emits events faster than persistence can absorb them.
- What invariants must hold during engine exit, reader failure, socket cleanup, and persistence failure.
- Whether the implementation should use native Asupersync surfaces directly or a local owned-worker design with equivalent semantics.

Why this matters:
Without this contract, implementation work risks replacing one vague concurrency shape with another.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The design specifies explicit ownership for listener, connection handling, persistence, and shutdown responsibilities.
2. The design specifies a bounded work-admission/backpressure policy and makes overflow behavior a deliberate product decision.
3. The design defines the lifecycle API exposed to `EngineDispatcher`, including stop/join/error semantics.
4. The design states clearly whether native Asupersync primitives are required at this seam or whether equivalent local structured semantics are sufficient.
5. The design names the invariants that the regression suite must later prove.","notes":"Use Asupersync concepts as the evaluation lens: owned work, bounded budgets/backpressure, explicit obligations, deterministic shutdown, and least-surprise failure semantics.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:41.680480829Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.914987857Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","design","runtime","telemetry"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.914931602Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:41.680480829Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1now.4.2","title":"Refactor TelemetryBridge listener and connection handling into owned worker topology","description":"Why this exists:
The accept loop and per-connection handling are currently modeled as nested detached threads. That shape makes lifecycle ownership implicit, complicates shutdown reasoning, and makes it easy to accumulate hidden concurrency without an explicit budget.

What this task must do:
Refactor TelemetryBridge so connection-handling work lives in an explicit owned topology rather than nested incidental threads.

Scope:
- Replace the current listener + nested per-connection thread fan-out with an owned-worker topology chosen in the design task.
- Ensure the topology makes concurrency and ownership explicit.
- Ensure connection cleanup and listener teardown are deterministic and inspectable.
- Avoid a design where accepted work can silently outlive the caller with no handle.

Why this matters:
This is the core structured-concurrency step in the telemetry migration.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The listener and connection-handling topology no longer rely on detached nested `thread::spawn` fan-out with no explicit ownership handle.
2. The chosen topology makes concurrency bounds and ownership explicit.
3. Connection cleanup, listener closure, and terminal states are deterministic enough to support regression testing.
4. The implementation matches the contract chosen by the design bead rather than inventing a new shape ad hoc.","notes":"This task is about work topology, not persistence ownership. Keep the queue/DB-owner decision separate so failures can be reasoned about cleanly.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:42.101522618Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.117426215Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","implementation","runtime","telemetry"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:42.101522618Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.117375530Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1now.4.3","title":"Replace telemetry shared-mutex persistence with bounded single-owner pipeline","description":"Why this exists:
The current telemetry path shares persistence through `Arc<Mutex<FrankensqliteAdapter>>`. That is simple, but it leaves ownership ambiguous and hides throughput/lock-contention/backpressure semantics behind a mutex.

What this task must do:
Replace the shared-mutex persistence path with an explicit bounded ingestion/persistence pipeline whose ownership and overflow behavior are deliberate.

Scope:
- Give one clearly defined worker/component ownership of persistence writes.
- Introduce an explicit bounded queue or equivalent admission-control mechanism between readers and persistence.
- Define what happens when persistence cannot keep up: block, shed, fail, or batch — but make it explicit.
- Preserve deterministic replay/audit correctness requirements while improving ownership clarity.

Why this matters:
Single-owner persistence is closer to Asupersync’s actor/obligation mindset and is much easier to reason about under failure than a shared mutex in a multi-threaded fan-out path.","acceptance_criteria":"1. `FrankensqliteAdapter` write ownership is explicit and no longer hidden behind a broadly shared `Arc<Mutex<...>>` in the telemetry ingestion hot path.
2. Ingestion uses bounded admission or an equally explicit alternative, with documented overflow/backpressure policy.
3. Persistence failure semantics are explicit and testable.
4. The change preserves audit/replay integrity while improving lifecycle clarity.","notes":"Do not choose an unbounded queue here unless the design task justifies why that is safer than explicit backpressure. Unbounded ingestion would erase one of the main advantages of doing this work.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:42.517609133Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.319695407Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","persistence","runtime","telemetry"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:42.517609133Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.319640615Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1now.4.4","title":"Wire EngineDispatcher to explicit telemetry lifecycle, shutdown, and error propagation","description":"Why this exists:
`EngineDispatcher` currently starts telemetry ingestion as a background side effect before launching the engine process. The caller does not receive an explicit lifecycle object describing how the bridge is stopped, joined, or observed for failure.

What this task must do:
Wire `EngineDispatcher` to an explicit telemetry lifecycle handle.

Scope:
- Starting telemetry must return or retain a concrete lifecycle handle/state object.
- Engine dispatch must define what happens to telemetry workers when engine startup fails, engine exit is normal, engine exit is abnormal, or telemetry itself fails.
- Socket cleanup must be deterministic and not depend on lucky process teardown ordering.
- Any stop/join/error path must match the contract from the design task.

Why this matters:
Even a perfectly designed worker topology is incomplete if the main dispatch path still treats it as an unmanaged side effect.","acceptance_criteria":"1. `EngineDispatcher` owns and resolves telemetry lifecycle explicitly rather than starting background work and forgetting it.
2. Startup-failure, engine-exit, and telemetry-failure paths are all defined and implemented.
3. Socket cleanup and worker shutdown are deterministic and observable.
4. The resulting dispatch path makes it possible to reason about “who is still running?” without guesswork.","notes":"This is the obligation-resolution bead for the telemetry cluster. Treat the lifecycle handle as something that must be resolved deliberately, not dropped casually.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:42.974073391Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.751335085Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","dispatcher","runtime","telemetry"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:42.974073391Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.518698934Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.751289971Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1now.4.5","title":"Add deterministic telemetry lifecycle, backpressure, and failure-forensics regression suite","description":"Why this exists:
A runtime-ownership refactor only pays off if the repo can prove the new lifecycle semantics under load, failure, and shutdown. Otherwise the code merely looks more structured while hiding the same bugs.

What this task must do:
Add a deterministic regression suite for telemetry ingestion lifecycle and failure behavior.

The suite should prove, at minimum:
- listener startup and shutdown sequencing,
- socket cleanup after normal and abnormal engine termination,
- bounded/backpressure behavior under event bursts,
- persistence failure handling,
- no orphan workers or silently abandoned ingestion state after lifecycle resolution,
- stable enough artifacts/logs to diagnose failures later.

Why this matters:
This is the evidence bead that converts the telemetry migration from a stylistic refactor into a correctness/reliability improvement.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The telemetry cluster has deterministic tests covering startup, shutdown, abnormal termination, backpressure/overflow, and persistence failure.
2. The tests prove that no owned worker silently outlives its lifecycle boundary after stop/join completes.
3. Failure artifacts/logs are sufficient for root-cause triage without rediscovering the architecture from scratch.
4. The suite is reliable enough to act as a regression gate for future lifecycle changes.","notes":"If native Asupersync lab/runtime surfaces are not introduced directly here, the tests should still aim for deterministic control over clocks, inputs, and shutdown order.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:43.403136908Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.949444416Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","runtime","telemetry","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:43.403136908Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.949398199Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1now.5","title":"Decide local semantic twin policy versus canonical franken_engine/asupersync adapters","description":"Why this exists:
`franken_node` currently contains local semantic twins of several Asupersync-flavored concepts: `ControlPlaneCx`, region ownership trees, supervision trees, performance budget guards, and other deterministic runtime kernels. Meanwhile, `frankenengine-engine` already centralizes canonical `/dp/asupersync` primitive imports.

What this task must do:
Create the policy that tells future contributors which local semantic twins should stay local, which should eventually collapse onto canonical upstream imports/adapters, and which should remain deferred until a live runtime boundary exists.

This task must inventory and classify at least:
- `connector/region_ownership.rs`
- `connector/supervision.rs`
- `runtime/region_tree.rs`
- performance budget guards
- any other node-side semantic twin discovered during the audit

Classification outcomes must be explicit, for example:
- keep-local model/spec surface,
- local wrapper over canonical upstream primitive,
- migrate to canonical import when boundary becomes live,
- forbidden duplicate/fork of an upstream-owned primitive.

Why this matters:
Without this policy, future work will oscillate between duplicate-type sprawl and premature migration churn.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The repo has an explicit inventory of local Asupersync-like semantic twins in `franken_node`.
2. Each inventoried surface has a clear policy outcome: keep local, wrap canonical, migrate later under trigger conditions, or forbid as a duplicate primitive fork.
3. The policy preserves the existing upstream ownership boundary in `frankenengine-engine` and avoids accidental duplication of canonical `/dp/asupersync` primitives.
4. Future contributors can use the policy to decide whether a new runtime concept belongs locally or upstream.","notes":"This bead is a semantic-boundary task, not a mandate to rewrite everything. A successful outcome may explicitly keep several node-side surfaces local.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:43.827371396Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:47.157280552Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","boundaries","runtime"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:43.827371396Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:47.157217855Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1now.6","title":"Deferred trigger: adopt native Asupersync request-region boundaries when a real async server lands","description":"Why this exists:
The audit deliberately rejected an immediate native-Asupersync migration of the control-plane service skeleton because `crates/franken-node/src/api/service.rs` is still a skeleton/catalog layer rather than a live async HTTP or gRPC server boundary.

What this deferred task must preserve for future self:
If `franken_node` later grows a real async network boundary, that is the moment to revisit native Asupersync request-region/capability-narrowing patterns.

Future trigger conditions:
- real socket accept loop or gRPC call boundary is introduced in this crate,
- request cancellation and per-request lifecycle become real runtime concerns,
- middleware begins owning spawn/time/IO authority rather than just metadata,
- the crate needs deterministic request shutdown/drain semantics.

When the trigger fires, the future implementation should evaluate:
- request-region ownership,
- narrowed request `Cx` capability exposure,
- explicit lifecycle/Outcome mapping at transport boundaries,
- whether the crate should own an `AppSpec`-like supervised service topology.

Why this bead exists now:
It prevents future contributors from forgetting that the right answer for HTTP/gRPC boundaries may eventually be native Asupersync — while also making clear that today is not that day.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The deferred bead captures the exact trigger conditions that would justify revisiting native Asupersync request/call boundary patterns in `franken_node`.
2. The bead makes it explicit that the current service skeleton is not yet sufficient justification for a migration.
3. Future contributors can pick this bead up without needing to rediscover why the work was deferred.","notes":"This bead should remain deferred until the crate owns a real async server boundary. Treat premature activation as a design smell.","status":"deferred","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:44.260873625Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:47.363467615Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","deferred-trigger","grpc","http","runtime"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.6","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:44.260873625Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.6","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:47.363398066Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1now.7","title":"Deferred trigger: promote singleton/mutex state owners only when topology justifies actor-style ownership","description":"Why this exists:
The audit found several singleton/mutex-backed or shared-owner surfaces in `franken_node` (for example shared fleet control manager and shared evidence ledger wrappers). Those do not automatically deserve actor/GenServer migration today, but some of them might if the concurrency topology becomes richer.

What this deferred task must preserve for future self:
Record the trigger conditions for promoting singleton/mutex-backed state owners into explicit actor/GenServer/service-lifecycle patterns.

Candidate surfaces to reevaluate later include:
- `api/fleet_quarantine.rs` shared fleet control manager,
- `observability/evidence_ledger.rs` shared ledger wrapper,
- any state owner that later gains reply obligations, named-worker semantics, restart domains, or meaningful concurrent mutation pressure.

Future trigger conditions:
- one natural owner is hidden behind broad shared mutable state,
- callers begin building ad hoc request/reply protocols around channels or locks,
- restart policy or failure isolation becomes important,
- lifecycle/ownership bugs become hard to reason about,
- contention or hidden coordination costs become measurable.

Why this bead exists now:
It gives future contributors a principled promotion rule instead of a style-war rule.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The deferred bead names the main singleton/shared-owner surfaces worth reevaluating later.
2. The bead captures the trigger conditions under which actor/GenServer/service-lifecycle promotion becomes justified.
3. The bead makes clear that today’s decision is “defer until topology demands it,” not “never improve these surfaces.”","notes":"Do not activate this bead just because a `Mutex` exists. Activate it when ownership, reply protocol, failure domain, or restart semantics become the real problem.","status":"deferred","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:44.680337102Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:47.586987448Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["actors","architecture","asupersync","deferred-trigger","runtime"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.7","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:44.680337102Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.7","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:47.586908040Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1o4v","title":"[10.18] Implement proof-verification gate API for control-plane trust decisions.","description":"## Why This Exists

Section 10.18 implements the Verifiable Execution Fabric (VEF) — Enhancement Map 9L — delivering proof-carrying runtime compliance. This is a Track C (Trust-Native Ecosystem Layer) critical gate that makes franken_node's trust decisions depend on cryptographic verification rather than trust-by-assertion.

This bead implements the proof-verification gate API — the component that control-plane trust decisions invoke to validate proofs before authorizing high-risk actions or trust transitions. The proof-generation service (bd-1u8m) produces proofs; this gate validates them. It is the trust boundary between "a proof exists" and "the proof is valid and binds to the correct policy and receipt window."

This gate is security-critical: it must be fail-closed (invalid or missing proofs default to denial), must validate the full binding chain (proof -> receipt-window commitment -> policy hash), and must produce stable verdict classes that downstream components (bd-8qlj for control transitions, bd-3pds for verifier SDK) can reason about deterministically.

## What This Must Do

1. Implement a verification gate API that accepts a proof envelope (from bd-1u8m) and validates it against the receipt-window commitment and policy hash binding.
2. Validate the full binding chain: proof envelope -> proof validity -> receipt-window commitment match -> policy hash match.
3. Return stable, classified verdict types: `VALID`, `INVALID_PROOF`, `MISSING_PROOF`, `STALE_POLICY`, `COMMITMENT_MISMATCH`, `VERIFICATION_ERROR`.
4. Enforce fail-closed semantics: any verification failure or missing input defaults to denial with a specific verdict class.
5. Support multiple proof backends transparently — the gate verifies proofs regardless of which backend generated them, using backend-specific verification parameters from the proof envelope.
6. Produce auditable verification records: every gate invocation (pass or fail) emits a signed, replayable verification record.
7. Expose a synchronous API suitable for hot-path integration in control-plane trust decisions.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Verification gate validates proof, receipt-window commitment, and policy hash binding; invalid/missing proofs return stable fail-closed verdict classes.
- All verdict classes are stable, enumerated, and documented with remediation guidance.
- Fail-closed behavior: missing proof -> `MISSING_PROOF` denial; malformed proof -> `INVALID_PROOF` denial; expired policy binding -> `STALE_POLICY` denial.
- Multi-backend support: proofs from different backends validate correctly through the same gate API.
- Verification records are signed and contain enough context for independent replay.
- Gate latency is bounded and suitable for synchronous control-plane integration.
- No silent pass-through: the gate never returns an affirmative verdict without completing full validation.

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests for each verdict class: valid proof, invalid proof, missing proof, stale policy, commitment mismatch, verification error.
- Fail-closed tests: null inputs, partial inputs, corrupted proof bytes — all must return denial verdicts.
- Multi-backend tests: validate proofs from the reference backend and any additional backends through the same gate.
- Binding chain tests: modify the receipt-window commitment after proof generation — must detect mismatch; modify policy hash — must detect stale policy.
- Replay tests: verification records contain sufficient context to re-execute the verification and reach the same verdict.
- Latency benchmark: measure gate verification time under load, confirm it stays within control-plane hot-path budget.
- Structured logging: `VEF-VERIFY-001` (verification requested), `VEF-VERIFY-002` (verdict issued), `VEF-VERIFY-ERR-*` (per verdict class).
- Trace correlation IDs linking verification requests to proof job IDs, receipt window ranges, and originating action contexts.

## Expected Artifacts

- `src/trust/vef_verification_gate.rs` — proof-verification gate API implementation.
- `tests/security/vef_verification_gate.rs` — security-focused tests for all verdict classes and fail-closed behavior.
- `artifacts/10.18/vef_verification_gate_report.json` — verification gate test report with verdict class coverage.
- `artifacts/section_10_18/bd-1o4v/verification_evidence.json` — machine-readable CI/release gate evidence.
- `artifacts/section_10_18/bd-1o4v/verification_summary.md` — human-readable summary linking intent to measured outcomes.

## Dependencies

- bd-1u8m (blocks) — Proof-generation service interface: produces the proof envelopes that this gate validates.

Dependents: bd-8qlj (VEF integration into control transitions), bd-3pds (verifier SDK integration), bd-2hjg (section gate), bd-32p (plan-level tracker).","acceptance_criteria":"- Verification gate validates proof, receipt-window commitment, and policy hash binding; invalid/missing proofs return stable fail-closed verdict classes.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:37:04.625740872Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T07:05:58.155167786Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T07:05:58.155130326Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-18","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1o4v","depends_on_id":"bd-1u8m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T17:05:51.460063573Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1ofy","title":"NaN fail-open in anti_amplification validate_policy bypasses ratio check","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-07T11:50:11.226584066Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-07T11:50:25.841977960Z","closed_at":"2026-03-07T11:50:25.841860601Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-1oju","title":"[10.4 bug] Bind trust cards and certification to verified evidence, not asserted trust inputs","description":"Why this exists:
The deeper 10.4 failure is downstream, not just at registry admission. `crates/franken-node/src/supply_chain/trust_card.rs` defines `TrustCardInput` as a bag of caller-supplied trust facts and then signs whatever it is given, while `crates/franken-node/src/supply_chain/certification.rs` evaluates `CertificationInput` from caller-supplied provenance/reputation/evidence fields instead of deriving them from verified upstream state. That means the project can mint polished, signed-looking trust cards and policy certification decisions from unverified assertions.

What this bead must do:
Bind trust-card materialization and certification to verified evidence handles, not free-form caller assertions. The design target is a typed trust pipeline: registry-admitted artifacts produce immutable verified evidence records; trust cards and certification consume those records by reference; every rendered trust claim is traceable back to canonical manifest/provenance/reputation/revocation evidence. Compile the abstruse parts into concrete artifacts: evidence IDs, append-only derivation receipts, minimal counterexample witnesses for disputed trust decisions, and deterministic replay bundles that let another verifier reconstruct why a card or certification level existed. Use the mathematically ambitious ideas only where they buy real leverage: Galois-style monotone trust derivations, lattice-safe demotion rules, and fraud-proof style witnesses for downstream trust disagreements.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Trust cards can no longer be created or mutated from raw caller-supplied provenance/reputation/certification claims; they must be derived from verified upstream evidence records or fail closed.
2. Certification evaluation consumes verified provenance/reputation/evidence references, not ad hoc booleans and free-form asserted levels.
3. Every trust card and certification result carries derivation metadata that links back to specific verified manifest/provenance/revocation/reputation inputs and survives deterministic replay.
4. Demotion and quarantine flows are monotone: once upstream evidence degrades or is revoked, downstream trust cards/certification cannot silently remain at the old level.
5. `bd-2yh`, `bd-273`, and `bd-y4g` share one canonical trust-derivation kernel instead of each accepting local asserted trust inputs.
6. Adversarial fixtures cover caller-injected fake provenance summaries, forged reputation jumps, stale certification evidence, conflicting upstream inputs, and trust-card mutation attempts that bypass verified evidence binding.
7. Add a checker that fails if replacement-critical trust surfaces reintroduce input structs or code paths that let callers inject final trust claims without verified evidence handles.
8. Deliver unit tests, integration/E2E trust-materialization fixtures, structured logs, and machine-readable derivation artifacts proving that downstream trust objects are grounded in verified state.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Trust-card and certification APIs consume verified evidence handles only; callers cannot supply final trust facts, provenance summaries, or certification levels directly on any replacement-critical path.
2. Downstream trust behavior is user-explainable: every trust card or certification result carries a derivation tree, downgrade and quarantine reasons, and stable reason codes suitable for UI, CLI, and incident bundles.
3. Unit tests cover handle typing, monotone demotion rules, revocation propagation, conflicting upstream evidence resolution, stale evidence rejection, and regression checks against asserted-input shortcuts.
4. Integration tests exercise registry admit -> trust-card materialization -> certification -> revocation -> downgrade -> quarantine -> recovery, proving downstream state changes track upstream evidence exactly.
5. At least one deterministic operator E2E shell script under tests/e2e/ reproduces trust-card issuance, certification changes, revocation-driven demotion, and quarantine behavior with detailed logs and replayable fixtures.
6. Structured logs expose stable TRUST_DERIVATION_* and CERTIFICATION_DECISION_* event families with required fields: trace_id, evidence_ids, subject_id, previous_level, new_level, reason_code, and replay_bundle_id.
7. Evidence artifacts under artifacts/replacement_gap/bd-1oju/ include machine-readable derivation receipts, human-readable trust summaries, adversarial fixture indexes, and rch offload build IDs for the verification run.
8. Completion requires a static regression checker that fails if replacement-critical trust surfaces reintroduce free-form asserted-input structs or bypass verified evidence handles.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-09T00:19:43.346293372Z","created_by":"RoseMountain","updated_at":"2026-03-09T06:07:05.867335162Z","closed_at":"2026-03-09T06:07:05.867202615Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-optimization","extreme-optimization","gap-closure","replacement-critical","section-10-0","section-10-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1oju","depends_on_id":"bd-3hdn","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T00:20:36.815255369Z","created_by":"RoseMountain","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
